oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Citron-crested Cockatoos Fighting for Survival
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Waugh, David
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 2 (July 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Citron-crested Cockatoos Fighting for SurvivalThe white cockatoo with the distinctive orange crest found on the Indonesian island of Sumba, long regarded as a subspecies of the Yellow-crested or Sulphur-crested Cockatoo (Cacatua sulphurea), has recently risen in rank to be officially accepted as a separate species, the Citron-crested Cockatoo (Cacatua citrinocristata). Both species remain classified as 'Critically Endangered' in the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. Not only does the Citron-crested Cockatoo continue to suffer from(equivalent to about 800-1,320 mature individuals). This is considered to constitute a decline in the population size of more than 80% over the past three generations, or 43 years.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 2 (2022): Watchbird July 2022; 18-19
